food_name: Yong Tau Foo with Dry Noodles (8 Pieces)
aliases: Stuffed Tofu and Vegetables Dry Noodles

english: Stuffed Tofu and Vegetables Dry Noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yong Tau Foo. FOODON_03315671 ('noodle dish') is the closest parent class available in FoodOn as of 2024. A more specific term for stuffed tofu and vegetable noodle dishes does not exist in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — mixed noodle dish with tofu and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (composite dish, mixed) is the closest parent anchor available. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Yong Tau Foo. Assigned at composite dish level as the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Yong Tau Foo as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: noodles (bee hoon / yellow noodles / kway teow)

serving.description: 1 bowl (8 pieces Yong Tau Foo with dry noodles)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising 8 assorted stuffed tofu and vegetable pieces served with a portion of dry-tossed noodles or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Yong Tau Foo (dry) nutrient composition; supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite dish data and USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for tofu, fish paste, and vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB); Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exactly matches '8pcs Yong Tau Foo Dry'. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Yong Tau Foo (dry) composite entry and cross-validated against IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data. Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained where consistent with HPB/IMR ranges and adjusted where discrepancies exceed 10%. Energy 112.7 kcal/100g, protein 6.4 g, fat 5.1 g, carbohydrate 10.9 g are consistent with HPB Yong Tau Foo dry reference range.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli and mixed noodle dish analogues; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo dry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the weighted average of the dish's carbohydrate contributors: rice vermicelli (GI ~58, University of Sydney GI Database), tofu and vegetables (GI negligible, effectively 0), and dark soy sauce (minimal carbohydrate contribution). Weighted composite GI estimated at 52 (low category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 60.0) / 100 = 31.2, rounded to 31.6 accounting for mixed noodle variant. Carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 10.91 × 5.5 = 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100 g; estimated 1800 mg per 550 g serving approaches 78% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced dark and light soy sauce, omit chilli sauce, and limit tau pok pieces to reduce sodium intake toward the recommended target of less than 2300 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 10.9 g/100 g with estimated GI 52 (low); 550 g serving delivers approximately 60 g carbohydrate and GL ~31
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should choose rice vermicelli or konjac noodles over yellow noodles to lower the glycaemic index, increase the proportion of vegetables and tofu relative to noodles, and monitor total carbohydrate load per serving to maintain post-meal glucose within target range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.45 g/100 g (low); trans fat 0.04 g/100 g (trace); sodium 327 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease can include Yong Tau Foo dry as a heart-friendly meal by requesting reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, limiting fried tau pok to reduce saturated fat and advanced glycation end-product exposure, and choosing plant-based tofu and vegetable pieces over fish-paste-heavy items.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.45 g/100 g); anti-inflammatory vegetables (bitter gourd, lady's finger, kangkong); nightshade content (brinjal, chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute nightshade vegetables (brinjal, chilli) with non-nightshade options, limit deep-fried tau pok to reduce AGE exposure, and request reduced soy sauce to keep sodium below the RA-relevant threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo with Dry Noodles (8 Pieces) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese hawker dish consisting of eight assorted pieces of tofu, fish paste-stuffed vegetables, and beancurd products served over dry-tossed noodles or rice. Common pieces include stuffed bitter gourd, lady's finger (okra), brinjal (eggplant), tau pok (fried beancurd puff), and firm tofu, all filled with a seasoned fish paste mixture. In the dry preparation, the noodles — typically rice vermicelli (bee hoon), yellow noodles, or flat rice noodles (kway teow) — are tossed in a savoury dark soy sauce with sesame oil rather than served in a soup broth. The dish has a mild, savoury flavour with a slightly sweet dark sauce, tender-chewy noodle texture, and a variety of vegetable textures from crisp to soft. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 113 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 5.1 g fat (1.45 g saturated), 10.9 g carbohydrate, 0.9 g sugar, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 327 mg sodium. A standard 550 g serving delivers approximately 620 kcal and 60 g carbohydrate. The estimated glycaemic index is 52 (low category), making it a moderate glycaemic load meal. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content from soy sauce; patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request reduced sauce. For type 2 diabetes management, choosing lower-GI noodle options and increasing vegetable proportion improves glycaemic outcomes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compatible. It is a culturally significant dish in Singapore and Malaysia, commonly consumed at hawker centres and food courts for breakfast, lunch, or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exactly matches '8pcs Yong Tau Foo Dry'. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Yong Tau Foo (dry) composite reference and cross-validated against IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data. Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with HPB reference ranges and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Yong Tau Foo dry composite dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the above approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific composite dish preparation is not available in the approved sources consulted. potassium_mg value of 210.0 mg/100 g is an estimate derived from ingredient-level USDA FoodData Central data for kangkong, bitter gourd, lady's finger, and tofu, as noted in the input health_suitability rationale; this value should be confirmed against a direct composite dish measurement when available. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement for Yong Tau Foo dry does not exist in approved sources. Halal status is conditional on fish paste and sauce ingredients being sourced from halal-certified suppliers. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not resolve to the official HPB Foodo database; the correct HPB Foodo URL (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) has been substituted. Ontology match_type values corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 entries, as these are the closest parent categories available in each ontology system for this dish; the previous values 'broader' and 'analogous_estimate' were not in the permitted set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
